sentences.text: All right, to continue about the a so-called commanding officer of the staff kommando, the SK, the penal kommando.
His name was Joseph Eckhardt, E-C-K--H-A-R-D-T.
He was-- his rank was unterscharfuhrer.
I think that is a non-commissioned officer, something like that.
I don't- I don't know the equivalent ranks in English.
But that's what he was.
And he was a vicious, very vicious beast.
You know, as I said before, that the-- one was supposed to only have two days or three days to live down there, because this was-- that's what it was supposed to be, a penal colony [INAUDUIBLE].
People went in there to-- to get tortured and murdered.
And that's it.
That's the end of the story.
I've seen him do things like-- oh, there's one particular thing where apparently he, he came in to the barrack in front of everybody.
And while we were in roll call, roll call in that outside, outside the barrack, you know, between the wall and the barracks.
And he said to the block [NON-ENGLISH], or the block elder-- his name was Emil Betnarek.
He was- he had a red triangle.
He was of Polish origin, but I think that he also spoke a little German.
Political, a political prisoner.
And also the designation P-- the letter P was in there, that you knew.
F was French, P was this, and so on.
And he told them that he received information that we will have to get another 10 prisoners in there, but there's no place.
So he said get rid of them.
And this is established, because there are eyewitnesses to this, including this boy here.
Betnarek went and got 10 people out.
And there was a little fella down there, was maybe 15 or 16 years old.
And he said, please, do it fast.
And he said-- he laid down on a piece of wood.
And he took an ax handle.
And he said in accented Polish, [POLISH].
I make clean work.
And he killed him.
But apparently this wasn't fast enough to eliminate the 10 people.
So Mr.
Unterscharfuhrer Eckhardt said to him-- he was very, you know, he was very taken back.
It takes so long.
He said, I'll show you what.
So he went in there and he shot the other nine.
We heard nine shots, and he shot them.
That was Mr.
Eckhardt.
And he was found by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Professor Hermann Langbein, who still lives in Vienna.
And he tried to put him on trial.
And I was interviewed by the RCMP a few years ago about that incident.
And I gave two days deposition out there.
And apparently the courts in Vienna said that he was unable to stand trial because he had a stroke, or he was very sick.
And when Langbein said, well, I would like to send a doctor down there to have him examined.
No.
Took his own doctor's word.
Then, from what I've heard, lat-- I didn't hear that he died, because Langbein would have told me that that son of a bitch is dead.
He also had a dog that he trained to get under the testicles of prisoners.
He loved it.
Oh, [?
Wolfy ?], that was the name of the dog.
There was also a man that-- again, this is where my lucky streak continued, so far-- who was a prisoner.
Who was a Gentile.
Who was a communist.
Who came from Austria, from one of the districts-- not in Vienna, outside somewhere in upper-- upper Austria, something like that.
He used to be a train engineer.
You know, he was driving a locomotive.
And he was an Aryan.
He was a Gentile.
And Eckhardt made him-- not his confidant, but he made him his main servant, because he had to eat.
We didn't-- he didn't give a damn that we didn't get anything to eat.
So he had a hut down there that was built.
And there was a little stove in there with coals and all that.
And Sepp, which was that prisoner, cooked potatoes and bacon that he organized for him.
And-- not the prisoner, but the SS man, Eckhardt, organized for himself.
Where the hell did he get them from?
And he cooked his meals and he-- because, again, of my Viennese accent, he heard me talk, this Sepp, and he-- I was-- dest-- you know, I said, hey.
Don't worry.
I'll take care of you.
He liked me.
And I became his helper.
I peeled the potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were some guys from France that came into the Straf company.
And these guys were very well connected.
They were communists.
And there was a large influence of commun-- there were undergrounds.
There were communist undergrounds, and Austrian undergrounds, and socialist undergrounds, Polish undergrounds, and all that in the camp.
And we knew that these guys are going to get out.
And they decided, one day, to do something to Mr.
Eckhardt.
So since I did the peeling of the potatoes down there, I had access to where the dog was when he wasn't in action.
He had a chord down there, and the chord-- so I just loosened the chord.
They got the dog.
They killed the dog.
They skinned the dog.
I don't know where they did it.
And Mr.
Eckhardt received a wonderful meal two days later that was his dog.
He thought-- and Sepp said he organized it.
He went to the kitchen and they had a young cow down there.
And the guy-- he thought it was like veal.
Enjoyed every portion and portion of it.
Then a-- this I heard of.
I was not a witness to this-- was that they tried to find a way how to get rid of Eckhardt.
But you can't just say to an SS man, I don't want you anymore.
Apparently, a letter was sent in his name with his signature to Himmler.
This is as the story goes.
And one day, he disappeared from the camp.
We found out after the war that Eckhardt was sent to the Russian front, because apparently the letter said that he wants to serve the Fuhrer better than-- do more for the Fuhrer than he does here.
And he volunteers to go to the front.
Now, if he signs a letter like that and he sends it to Himmler, Himmler thinks he's the greatest thing after sliced bread.
So he sent him there.
But apparently, before he left-- the night before he left-- a letter was smuggled in to him, telling him that he ate his own dog.
I love that story.
It's a beautiful story.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
Penal colony?
Until January-- January-- February-- January or February, something like that.
I-- no, wait.
The sentence was three months.
But I was only there for a month or five weeks.
What happened was that one of the so-called-- and this is an expression-- a lot of names and words that were used in the camp were either German or Polish.
For instance, the dish that we ate out was a dish.
The Poles used to call them miske or miska.
And that's what everybody called it.
Even the Germans called it miska.
The so-called-- I told you the flunkies that worked for the block elder?
The German name for that is stubendienst.
OK?
But they were called [POLISH].
The-- the-- the-- the Poles coined a word and that's what they're called.
And there was one guy down there, and he was a really ugly brute.
And I think I didn't make my bed right.
The bed, the bunk, the straw was sticking out, and he hit me on my hand.
And next morning, my hand was like this.
It was black and blue and swollen like this and that.
And I couldn't touch anything.
It hurt that much.
When somebody says-- sometimes I look at my number.
Very often I look at my finger, my second finger, next to the little one, because there is an indentation down there of a surgery that was done in camp.
I went to the sick bay.
And there were two medical students down there.
I think one was from France for sure.
I think the other one was from either Norway or Denmark.
They weren't Jewish.
They were working in the hospital.
And they cut it open in two places, one here and one there.
And blood spurted out like crazy.
And then they bandaged it.
And instead of me being sent back to the SK, the two friends that I had that used to work in Kanada got me out of there.
And I ended up in the Kanada block, the barracks.
Because of Seppl.
He was that guy that protected me, because he didn't touch me.
And I never-- and this guy Eckhardt never even touched me.
Just ignored me.
And as a matter of fact, again because of the language that he heard me speak to Seppl.
I don't know.
I don't think he loved me, but he didn't do anything to me.
But he killed everybody else in sight.
Anyhow, I ended up in the barracks of Kanada.
And Kanada was the elite kommando down there, because they were the ones that were at the ramp when people arrived.
They were the ones that took the luggage that people had to leave either in the wagons or outside, and loaded it on a truck and brought it into a little satellite camp which was-- which Kanada had.
There were four or five barracks down there.
And the suitcases were opened, broken open or put open, and then everything was sorted out.
We found everything.
Everything under the sun was there.
Well it's-- if somebody tells you that all you can take is twenty kilo, and you're going to be resettled, what the hell are you going to take?
You're going to take food.
You're going to take a little clothing.
You take all your jewelry, all your money, everything you can lay your hand on.
That what was there.
OK?
Fine.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Kanada was something that was extremely awful, and at the same time life-saving.
The other thing is that you could see, when a transport arrived, who is going live and who is going to die, immediately.
What happened afterwards, after the people that got into the camp, that was a different story.
That you didn't know.
But you knew exactly who was going to live and die.
If ever there was something that hits me every time I talk about it or think about it-- and I think about it because of what happens in my daily life-- is the children.
You know, the people that arrived there arrived in cattle cars.
So well dressed people, poorly dressed people, one thing they all had in common they were very tired.
They were very thirsty.
We were not allowed to talk to them.
If you talked to them, somebody caught you, you were dead.
They took you right behind the ditch and killed you, because they didn't want us to pass information.
And again, you don't make yourself-- there are no big shots in the camp.
Oh, there were some heroes.
There were heroes there that I respect.
Most of them, 90% of the heroes, are dead.
But they were heroes.
But the rest, like me, me included, hey, I was no hero.
But you try to do whatever you could do.
Like, for instance, you saw these kids came off.
And you look at them.
And they were all ages.
They were from infants that were carried by parents to little boys and girls that were walking.
They [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had a little toy in their hands.
They were hanging on to their mothers.
They looked bewildered.
They looked frightened.
Some of them cried.
And you knew that one hour later these kids are all going to be dead.
You didn't, you know, somehow didn't focus in on the grownups.
And that bothers me until this day.
Why?
I live right next to a high school.
And there are a lot of daycare centers or kindergartens around us here.

And whenever I see-- when I'm in the street, not from here, from the balcony-- but when I'm in the street and I see these kids going with the, with their kindergarten teachers and all that, I just turn away.
Because it all comes back to me.
It's murder.
That's exactly what it was.
Anyhow--
No, always.
One child, two children, fine.
I see a bunch of children, I-- I remember.
It's, it's, it's, it's the trigger that's there.
You know, something triggers it.
And that's what it is.
And this is the-- see, I know that I'm going off the subject more or less now.
I'm talking about myself, that I have friends that were in camp to me.
I know they can't forget.
They certainly can't forgive.
But they are fortunate not to think about it.
I wake up with it in the morning.
And I go to bed with it at night.
There hasn't been an evening, even-- I don't drink, but I used to drink at parties.
And I used to get drunk.
Even when I got drunk, before I went off to sleep, I dreamt about Auschwitz.
I thought about Auschwitz, crazy things that came to me.
And it's always something else.
It's never the same.
Anyhow, that's neither here nor there.
So here I'm-- I'm at the-- at the incident, the major incident at Kanada.
Oh yes, yes.
As a matter of fact, I found my first love down there, Mancy, Mancy Neuman.
I couldn't talk to her, but I made-- I ogled her.
And I met her in later life.
And boy, she-- she-- she's-- well, she got older.
She looked different than she looked down there.
See, food was in abundance down there.
We were not allowed to eat, but every time we broke up a suitcase-- maybe it was an unwritten law.
You didn't walk around with a sandwich with a piece of bread in your hand.
But you just stuffed-- it's like being invisible.
It's like eating invisibly.
You didn't chew.
You put it in your mouth and you swallowed it.
It digested itself.
We had amusing things down there.
And we had very sad things down there.
I guess that's life.
The sad things that especially happened to me was that one day I opened up a suitcase and I saw a picture in there of my cousin that I haven't seen since 1937.
Apparently she went to Italy, to Trieste, in 1937, before Hitler.
And I looked.
There's a picture there, right on top of it.
It's her, because she had a very-- I don't know.
She had very crinkly hair.
It looked like an Afro almost, you know.
And so I look at her and she has two children on the side, one left, one on the right.
And I started to cry.
I really did.
That was the only time that I cried in Auschwitz.
And I have cried very often after that.
I never cried in Auschwitz but at that point.
Rosa.
I don't know her second name.
I mean, she was married.
Her first name is-- what she used to be-- Sass, S-A-S-S.
And all of a sudden, somebody hits me on the head.
I turn around, Unterscharfuhrer Hans-- that's all I knew him under, as Hans, H-A-N-S.
Again, non-commissioned officer.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What are you doing?
[GERMAN].
Bingo, another one.
I said, well, herr unterscharfuhrer, this is my cousin.
Ah, the cousin, cousin, cousin, arbeit.
Work.
I pick up a little pouch, a little velvet pouch.
It couldn't have been bigger than about that, maybe six inches, five inches, with a little string on there.
I open it up-- and just to predicate this statement, at the one end of the barracks where we broke open the suitcases, there was a big wooden box down there with two handles.
And every piece of jewelry, monies, anything that was of value had to be dumped in down there.
The clothing, the chemises, the panties, the who knows what, the blankets-- everybody brought a blanket anyhow-- were put inside down there.
And then they were brought over to the girls', to the women's barrack down there, where they had to catalog it and put it so it had to be sent to Germany if it was clean and all that.
And so I opened that little pouch and I put it on my hand.
And there were two rings down there with some stones in there.
I don't know, some red stones.
And a small bracelet with a few stones in there.
And I want to take this.
And I'm going to put it into that, into that box.
And all of a sudden, Hans says to me, says [GERMAN].
Just give it to me.
I'll throw it in down there, OK?
So I give it to him.
And just as much as I observed what happened outside crematorium five in 1944, it's like having eyes in the back.
I saw that he did not.
He walked toward that, but he never dropped it in.
I saw him putting it in his tunic.
That evening, when roll call-- again, the roll-- all roll call was always there.
We were counted again in fives.
And sometimes they used to search us, because we weren't allowed to take anything out.
We had no pockets.
But we found certain ways.
And if they didn't search us, we got it out.
So he comes to me, and he, uh, he-- spread.
So I put my legs apart and my, my, my hand, my, my arms up like this.
And he goes with his stick, never with his hand.
And he goes my arms, and my sides, between my legs and my crotch.
Nothing.
I didn't take anything.
Not that I'd never took anything, but that day I didn't take anything.
And all of a sudden he looks at my shoes.
There was an unwritten law down there.
When you walked in in the morning, you walked in with wooden shoes.
When you walked out, you had the finest shoes that you can find.
And they knew it.
They never said anything.
Shoes were used either to give to your friends-- and you had lots of friends, especially when you worked in Kanada-- or you used as a trading material.
You used to give it to somebody that you knew that is going to help you if you needed help, no matter if the guy was the biggest murderer in the whole goddamn camp.
It was one of the guys that I hooked up with that saved my life, because I gave him something every occasion I found blah blah blah.
I didn't need it.
He said, that isn't camp. this is [GERMAN].
That's not a camp issue.
I said no, sir.
He said, take them off.
And here, I'm standing here with all these guys.
Everybody has shoes on.
I take them off.
I take them off.
Bend over.
That's when I got my next 25.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I was in bad shape.
They, my friends-- we were all friends-- half carried me, half dragged me back to the camp, which was quite a ways off, into Birkenau.
And the block elders, the block elder down there was bribed every time the kommando came back.
And he treated us wonderfully.
He was also a criminal.
He had a green triangle.
But, you know, this was the, the, the, the block of the, the, the high and mighty, Kanada kommando.
And so he said, you're not going to work tomorrow.
You can't go to work tomorrow.
You can't even crawl.
So I stayed in the barracks for two days.
And then I came back.
This was in April when that happened.
And I knew that prior to this, there were a couple of transports that came from Belgium.
And you had a choice that not you made, but you could arrange it.
Let's put it this way.
Either you worked in that satellite camp where they opened the luggage, or you went to the ramp when a transport arrived.
OK?
Not the whole kommando, though, all the-- not the whole detail went to the ramp, just part of it.
OK?
And when I saw that, I don't know, not a hunch, not a feeling.
I just went to the ramp every chance that I got.
And one day, on the 22nd of April, 1943, I saw a transport come in.
And saw my parents get off.
And saw my father walk up to the SS men with the others.
Sent to the left, then my mother the same thing.
She was sent to the left, and that's the last I saw of them.
I also read--
No.
Oh, no.
It was a huge place, a very long place.
There were-- must have been about 15 or 16 cars.
You know, we were spread out all over the place.
But I saw them.
OK, my father was-- let's see now-- 21-- 60-- 64.
And my mother was 52.
She was 12 years younger.
But, you know, in retrospect, I told you I only cried once.
Funny enough-- when I saw the picture-- I didn't cry.
First thing that I did was, when I went back to the camp, there was a holy man down there.
And I don't mean this disparagingly.
There was a man that came in that was some wunder rabbi or something like that.
And, as I call-- we always used to call them-- the boys, got together and said this guy's going to die, because first of all he doesn't want to eat anything.
It's trafe.
He doesn't want to eat it.
So he only lived on bread.
And water, there was no water.
So bread and soup had to be given.
And then they had to find a place for him to work.
So where did they put him?
They put him in the most awful work detailed that existed in the camp that wasn't-- not heavy.
It was heavy enough, but it wasn't heavy.
But nobody touched him.
He used to be called a scheisse kommando, if you know my inference.
Well, you used to have open toilets down there, you know.
So you had to have somebody that took all that feces and all that crap out there and put it into a truck.
And it splashed on them.
And you know how they smelled.
So everybody stayed away.
That's where this guy worked.
I went up to him and I said to him, my parents were killed today.
What's that date in the Jewish calendar?
Because I did not know this was the 22nd.
I didn't have a clue.
I mean, I didn't have a calendar with me.
And he said, today is the 30th of Pesach-- Passover.
That's how I found that it was the 22nd.
And then when I had the information that the transport left on the 20th from Belgium, later on, then I knew that this was the right date.
And that's where I have my yahrzeit.
You know what that is?
OK?
That I don't have to translate, I don't think.
And then things became very tricky.
Tricky in such a way that I got scared that this guy is going to get me.
This guy, Hans Kuehneman.
So I tried to get out of that place.
And you can't just walk away from it.
They have to go through the, uh, the office of the people that put the work kommandos together and all that, just like an office.
I mean, it wasn't an office.
The office was outside.
And I went to my friend-- another-- you know, funny enough, this is the third Seppl.
Seppl.
Means Joseph.
It's like Dolphy to Adolf.
The real name is Joseph, and the abbreviation in Bavaria and in Austria is Seppl, S-E-P-P-L, Or Sepp, S-E-P-P.
And this guy was a-- a murderer.
He was a-- a-- a [GERMAN], in other words, a green triangle.
He came from jail somewhere.
That's how they brought these kapos in there in the beginning.
And because again, of his dialect-- he came from Bavaria-- this is how I discovered him, and he discovered me.
And I brought him something.
I brought him a ring.
And I brought him a piece of soap.
And I brought him a handkerchief.
Who the hell knows what?
Something that I could hide.
We became pals.
I visited him in his barracks down there, where everybody was frightened to death.
But he knew they were going to bring him something.
And I kept him as an insurance.
And that's exactly what he was.
So I went to him one day after my feelings of saying this guy's going to-- he's gonna get me.
I told him so two years ago, because he was put on trial.
We found him.
That guy Johannes Kuehneman, Hans.
And I said, I have to get out.
He wasn't very pleased when he heard that, because his source would dry up.
I said, look, Seppl, I have friends in Kanada.
Every once in while, you're going to get something.
In other words, you know, I put a selling job on this guy.
So he was the kapo of the roofers.
And the roofers went all over the place.
They went to the women's camp.
They went to the gypsy camp.
They went to a camp that was called Mexico, for all intents and purposes, where people were dying like flies.
They went-- I repeat myself-- where the women were, and all that kind of thing.
And they fixed roofs in the barracks.
He was famous for killing people by dropping a nail.
If a guy dropped a nail from the roof down there, he went up there and hit him over the head and killed him.
That was Seppl.
After this I saw Seppl again, in 1944, but that's not it.
Now, so he--
Oh, sure, I worked for him.
And boy, I tell you, when-- first of all, the boys knew what I have done, because I told them that's what I'm going to do.
And I became a roofer.
